title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: R Quick Reference
text:
This page summarizes R code from the tidyverse from our class.











Step
Code idea
Quick check




1
library(tidyverse)
Packages load without errors


2
read_csv(...); glimpse(dat)
Column names and types match Part 1


3
mutate(... factor(..., levels = ...))
Levels match your two groups / outcome categories


4
group_by + summarise and/or count
Means/SDs if response is numerical; counts (and optional props) if categorical


5
ggplot
Boxplot/histogram for numerical response by group; bar chart of counts for binary/categorical (L25–27)


6
Test + CI
t.test(..., var.equal = FALSE) or prop.test / chisq.test per Methods table


7
Short interpretation in prose
p-value addresses chance only; causal language only if design supports it









Code
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}
library(tidyverse)













Task
Code pattern




Load CSV
dat <- read_csv("file.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)


Peek structure
glimpse(dat)


First rows
head(dat, 10)














Task
Code pattern




Set levels / order
dat <- dat \|> mutate(group = factor(group, levels = c("A", "B")))


Check levels
levels(dat$group)



Use factors so ggplot and tables order categories sensibly.











Setting
Code pattern




One numerical variable
dat \|> summarize(mean_y = mean(y), sd_y = sd(y), n_y=n())


Numerical by two groups
dat \|> group_by(group) \|> summarize(n = n(), mean_y = mean(y), sd_y = sd(y))


Counts for one categorical
dat \|> count(category) \|> mutate(prop = n / sum(n))


2 × 2 counts
dat \|> count(group, outcome) then pivot_wider














Setting
Pattern




Numerical response, two groups
ggplot(dat, aes(x = group, y = y)) + geom_boxplot() (Lecture 25); or geom_histogram(aes(fill = group), ...)


Two categorical (e.g. group × outcome)
dat \|> count(group, outcome) \|> ggplot(aes(x = group, y = n, fill = outcome)) + geom_col() (Lecture 27); add position = "fill" for 100% stacked


Two numerical
ggplot(dat, aes(x = x1, y = x2)) + geom_point() (Lecture 26)


Subclassification (confounder)
Add facet_wrap(~ confounder) to the plot above (Lecture 28)



Always add labs(title = ..., x = ..., y = ...) with units where relevant.




Use the Lecture 30 Methods table to pick one primary test for the “by chance” question.








Response type
R command
Notes




Numerical, 2 groups
t.test(y ~ group, data = dat, var.equal = FALSE)
Welch t-test; output includes 95% CI for \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\) (Lecture 29)


Binary (0/1 or two levels), 2 groups
prop.test(x = c(x1, x2), n = c(n1, n2), correct = FALSE)
Compares two proportions


Two categorical variables
chisq.test(tab)
Check chisq.test(tab)$expected for ≥ 5 rule (Lecture 28)














Task
Example pattern




Build tibble
tibble(group = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30), y = c(rnorm(30, 10, 2), rnorm(30, 12, 2)))


Binary outcome
rbinom(n, 1, prob = ...) inside mutate or c(...)


Save
write_csv(dat, "project4_data.csv")





read_csv path and glimpse — Do column names match your Part 1 definitions?
factor — Are group and outcome coded with the correct levels?
group_by + summarize — Does the code compute means/SDs for numerical responses and counts/proportions for categorical responses?
ggplot — Is the geom right (boxplot vs bar vs histogram) for your variable types?
Test — Is it t.test(..., var.equal = FALSE) for mean vs prop.test / chisq.test for proportions?
Conditions — random sample or assignment? sample size large enough? See methods table for exact condition to check.
